title: CESF: A Controllable Error Synthesis Framework for Reproducible Data Cleaning Evaluation
description: We propose CESF, a deterministic error synthesis framework that generates tabular datasets with controlled, reproducible errors for benchmarking data cleaning algorithms. Unlike stochastic LLM-based generators or limited rule-based tools like BART, CESF provides fine-grained control over error types (syntax, constraint violations, semantic anomalies), error rates, and coverage dimensions while guaranteeing deterministic output through seeded generation. The framework includes a validated error taxonomy covering 8 error types that account for 86% of real-world errors and provides four quantitative coverage metrics for benchmark quality assessment.
motivation: Data cleaning research lacks reproducible benchmarks. Existing error generators like BART (2015) are limited to simple constraint violations and random character mutations, failing to capture realistic error patterns. Recent LLM-based approaches (TableEG 2025) produce more diverse errors but are non-deterministic, making fair algorithm comparison impossible. We need a framework that provides both expressiveness (diverse, realistic errors) and determinism (reproducible, controllable experiments). Simply extending BART with more error types is insufficient because BART's architecture was designed for constraint violations, not systematic coverage of diverse error types, and it lacks coverage metrics and validation against real-world error distributions.
proposed_approach: CESF operates through three components: (1) A formal error taxonomy defining 8 error classes spanning syntactic (typos, formatting, whitespace), structural (FD violations, DC violations, key violations), and semantic (outliers, implausible values) dimensions, validated to cover 86% of real-world errors in Hospital, Flights, Food, and Adult datasets; (2) A constraint-aware error injection engine that deterministically corrupts clean datasets based on configurable parameters (error rate per class, coverage requirements, seed values); (3) A coverage analyzer that measures benchmark completeness through four metrics: type coverage, distribution balance, detectability score, and repair difficulty index. The framework is fully deterministic—identical configurations produce identical outputs—enabling reproducible experiments while supporting systematic coverage of the error space.

related_work.title: Error Generation for Evaluating Data-Cleaning Algorithms
related_work.authors: Arocena, Glavic, Mecca, Miller, Papotti, Santoro
related_work.difference: BART provides deterministic error generation but is limited to denial constraints and simple random mutations (character swaps, deletions). CESF extends this with a validated taxonomy of 8 semantic error classes, systematic coverage metrics, and fine-grained controllability. Unlike BART, CESF validates its error types against real-world datasets achieving 86% coverage.

related_work.title: On Generating Authentic Errors via Large Language Models
related_work.difference: TableEG uses fine-tuned LLMs to generate realistic errors but sacrifices determinism. CESF provides deterministic guarantees while approximating error diversity through structured error taxonomies and combinatorial synthesis. CESF also provides coverage metrics absent in LLM-based approaches.

related_work.title: REIN: A Comprehensive Benchmark Framework for Data Cleaning Methods in ML Pipelines
related_work.difference: REIN uses BART for error injection and focuses on ML pipeline evaluation. CESF provides a more expressive and controllable error generation mechanism with validated taxonomies that could enhance frameworks like REIN.

related_work.title: HoloClean: Holistic Data Repairs with Probabilistic Inference
related_work.difference: HoloClean is a repair system, not a benchmark generator. CESF enables systematic evaluation of repair systems like HoloClean by providing reproducible test cases with known ground truth.

related_work.title: Cleaning Relations Using Knowledge Bases
related_work.difference: Hao et al. focus on knowledge base-guided data cleaning. CESF focuses on error synthesis for benchmarking, which could generate test cases for evaluating KB-based cleaning approaches.

related_work.title: Outlier Identification in Heterogeneous Datasets Using Automatic Transformation
related_work.difference: dBoost is an outlier detection tool, not an error generator. CESF can generate outliers as one of its 8 error types for benchmarking outlier detection methods like dBoost.

hypothesis: A deterministic error synthesis framework with formal error taxonomies validated against real-world error distributions (covering 86% of errors) and controllable coverage metrics (type coverage, distribution balance, detectability, repair difficulty) enables more reproducible and comprehensive evaluation of data cleaning algorithms compared to existing stochastic or limited rule-based approaches.
success_criteria: CESF generates reproducible errors: identical seeds produce identical corrupted datasets (verified through hash comparison)
Taxonomy validation: 8 error types cover ≥85% of errors in real-world dirty datasets (Hospital, Flights, Food)
All 4 coverage metrics implemented and validated: type coverage, distribution balance, detectability score, repair difficulty index
Benchmarking experiments on standard datasets (Hospital, Flights, Food) show differentiated performance profiles across cleaning algorithms
Determinism enables fair comparison: running the same experiment multiple times yields identical rankings of cleaning algorithms
CESF achieves >20% larger spread in algorithm F1 scores compared to BART (better discrimination)
venue_target: VLDB, SIGMOD, or ICDE (database systems venues)
computational_requirements: CPU-only, no GPU required. Experiments involve dataset generation and running existing cleaning algorithms on standard benchmarks.
